content.title: Sustainable architecture - lessons from the ant

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Africa owes its termite mounds a lot. Trees and shrubs take root in them. Prospectors mine them, looking for specks of gold carried up by termites from hundreds of metres below. And of course, they are a special treat to aardvarks and other insectivores.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Now, Africa is paying an offbeat tribute to these towers of mud. The extraordinary Eastgate Building in Harare, Zimbabwe's capital city, is said to be the only one in the world to use the same cooling and heating principles as the termite mound.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Termites in Zimbabwe build gigantic mounds inside which they farm a fungus that is their primary food source. This must be kept at exactly 30.5°C, while the temperatures on the African veld outside can range from 1.5°C at night only just above freezing to a baking hot 40°C during the day. The termites achieve this remarkable feat by building a system of vents in the mound. Those at the base lead down into chambers cooled by wet mud carried up from water tables far below, and others lead up through a flue to the peak of the mound. By constantly opening and closing these heating and cooling vents over the course of the day the termites succeed in keeping the temperature constant in spite of the wide fluctuations outside.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Architect Mick Pearce used precisely the same strategy when designing the Eastgate Building, which has no air conditioning and virtually no heating. The building the country's largest commercial and shopping complex uses less than 10% of the energy of a conventional building ns size. These efficiencies translated directly to the bottom line: the Eastgate's owners saved $3.5 million on a $36 million building because an air- conditioning plant didn't have to be imported. These savings were also passed on to tenants: rents are 20% lower than in a new building next door.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: The complex is actually two buildings linked by bridges across a shady, glass-roofed atrium open to the breezes. Fans suck fresh air in from the atrium, blow it upstairs through hollow spaces under the floors and from there into each office through baseboard vents. As it rises and warms, it is drawn out via ceiling vents and finally exits through forty- eight brick chimneys.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: To keep the harsh, highveld sun from heating the interior, no more than 25% of the outside is glass, and all the windows are screened by cement arches that just out more than a metre.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: During summer's cool nights, big fans flush air through the building seven times an hour to chill the hollow floors. By day, smaller fans blow two changes of air an hour through the building, to circulate the air which has been in contact with the cool floors. For winter days, there are small heaters in the vents.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: This is all possible only because Harare is 1600 feet above sea level, has cloudless skies, little humidity and rapid temperature swings days as warm as 31°C commonly drop to 14°C at night.'You couldn't do this in New York, with its fantastically hot summers and fantastically cold winters,' Pearce said. But then his eyes lit up at the challenge.' Perhaps you could store the summer's heat in water somehow.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: The engineering firm of Ove Amp & Partners, which worked with him on the design, monitors daily temperatures outside, under the floors and at knee, desk and ceiling level. Ove Arup's graphs show that the temperature of the building has generally stayed between 23"C and 25°C. with the exception of the annual hot spell just before the summer rains in October, and three days in November, when a janitor accidentally switched off the fans at night. The atrium, which funnels the winds through, can be much cooler. And the air is fresh far more so than in air-conditioned buildings, where up to 30% of the air is recycled.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: Pearce, disdaining smooth glass skins as 'igloos in the Sahara', calls his building, with its exposed girders and pipes, 'spiky'. The design of the entrances is based on the porcupine-quill headdresses of the local Shona tribe. Elevators are designed to look like the mineshaft cages used in Zimbabwe's diamond mines. The shape of the fan covers, and the stone used in their construction, are echoes of Great Zimbabwe, the ruins that give the country its name.

paragraphs.label: K
paragraphs.text: Standing on a roof catwalk, peering down inside at people as small as termites below. Pearce said he hoped plants would grow wild in the atrium and pigeons and bats would move into it. like that termite fungus, further extending the whole 'organic machine' metaphor. The architecture, he says, is a regionalised style that responds to the biosphere, to the ancient traditional stone architecture of Zimbabwe's past, and to local human resources.

questions.title: Why do termite mounds have a system of vents?

options.text: to allow the termites to escape from predators
to enable the termites to produce food
to allow the termites to work efficiently
to enable the termites to survive at night

questions.analysis: 这道题要求我们找出为什么白蚁丘会有一个通风系统。我们可以通过定位词"termite mounds"在原文中找到相关信息。原文中提到，津巴布韦的白蚁在丘陵内种植一种真菌，这是它们的主要食物来源。为了保持真菌的温度恒定在30.5°C，白蚁在丘陵中建造了一个通风系统。这个系统由通向丘陵底部的通风口和通向丘陵顶部的通风口组成。通过不断开启和关闭这些通风口，白蚁成功地保持了温度的稳定。因此，答案是选项B，即为了使白蚁能够生产食物。

central_sentences.sentence: The termites achieve this remarkable feat by building a system of vents in the mound
central_sentences.translation: 白蚁通过在土堆中建造通风系统来实现这一非凡的壮举。

questions.title: Why was Eastgate cheaper to build than a conventional building?

options.text: Its energy consumption was so low.
Its tenants contributed to the costs.

questions.analysis:
题干要求我们找出为什么Eastgate的建筑成本比传统建筑要便宜的原因。我们可以通过定位词和同义替换的方法来快速找到答案。

首先，我们可以看到题干中的定位词是"Eastgate"。在原文中，Eastgate被同义替换为"Eastgate Building"。在原文中，有一句话提到了Eastgate Building的设计策略："Architect Mick Pearce used precisely the same strategy when designing the Eastgate Building, which has no air conditioning and virtually no heating."（建筑师Mick Pearce在设计Eastgate Building时采用了完全相同的策略，该建筑没有空调，几乎没有供暖设备。）

接下来，我们需要找到关于建筑成本的信息。题干中的定位词是"cheaper"，在原文中，这个同义替换为"saved $3.5 million"。原文中有一句话提到了Eastgate Building的节能效果带来的成本节约："These efficiencies translated directly to the bottom line: the Eastgate’s owners saved $3.5 million on a $36 million building because an air-conditioning plant didn't have to be imported."（这些效率直接转化为底线：Eastgate的业主在一座价值3600万美元的建筑上节省了350万美元，因为不需要进口空调设备。）

最后，我们需要找到关于传统建筑的信息。题干中的定位词是"conventional building"，在原文中，这个同义替换为"a conventional building ns size"。原文中有一句话提到了Eastgate Building相比传统建筑的能源使用效率："The building the country's largest commercial and shopping complex uses less than 10% of the energy of a conventional building ns size."（这座国家最大的商业和购物综合体的建筑使用的能源不到传统建筑的10%。）

综上所述，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出答案："No air conditioners were needed."（不需要空调设备）。因此，正确答案是D选项。

questions.title: Why would a building like Eastgate not work efficiently in New York?

options.text: Temperature change occurs seasonally rather than daily.
Pollution affects the storage of heat in the atmosphere.
Summer and winter temperatures are too extreme.
Levels of humidity affect cloud coverage.

questions.analysis:
这道题要求我们找出为什么像Eastgate这样的建筑在纽约不能高效工作。我们可以通过对比原文中的信息来找到答案。

首先，我们可以看到题干中提到的"building like Eastgate"在原文中被表达为"this"。原文中提到，这种建筑的实现是因为哈拉雷位于海拔1600英尺，天空晴朗，湿度较低，温度变化剧烈。然后，作者提到在纽约这样的地方是无法实现这种建筑的，因为纽约夏天非常炎热，冬天非常寒冷。

因此，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出答案是A选项："Temperature change occurs seasonally rather than daily."，即温度变化是季节性的，而不是每天都发生变化。

central_sentences.sentence: This is all possible only because Harare is 1600 feet above sea level, has cloudless skies, little humidity and rapid temperature swings days as warm as 31°C commonly drop to 14°C at night.‘You couldn’t do this in New York, with its fantastically hot summers and fantastically cold winters,’ Pearce said
central_sentences.translation: 这一切之所以可能，完全是因为哈拉雷位于海拔1600英尺的高地，拥有晴朗的天空、较低的湿度和快速的温度变化。白天温度常常达到31摄氏度，而夜晚则会降至14摄氏度。皮尔斯说：“在纽约这样夏季炎热、冬季寒冷的地方是做不到这一点的。”

questions.title: What does Ove Arup's data suggest about Eastgate's temperature control system?

options.text: It allows a relatively wide range of temperatures.
The only problems are due to human error.
It functions well for most of the year.
The temperature in the atrium may fall too low

questions.analysis:
根据题干"What does Ove Arup’s data suggest about Eastgate’s temperature control system?"，我们需要找到Ove Arup的数据对于Eastgate的温度控制系统的建议。我们可以通过以下步骤快速找到答案：

1. 首先，我们注意到题干中提到了"Ove Arup’s data"，我们可以在原文中寻找与之相关的信息。原文中提到了"Ove Amp & Partners"，这是与Ove Arup相关的工程公司。

2. 接下来，我们需要找到Ove Arup的数据所显示的关于建筑物温度的信息。原文中提到了"Ove Arup's graphs"，这些图表显示了建筑物的温度范围。

3. 根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出结论，Eastgate的温度控制系统在大部分时间内运行良好。原文中提到，建筑物的温度通常保持在23°C至25°C之间，除了每年夏季降雨前的热浪和11月的三天，当时一个看门人在夜间意外关闭了风扇。

综上所述，根据Ove Arup的数据，我们可以得出结论，Eastgate的温度控制系统在大部分时间内运行良好。因此，答案是"C. It functions well for most of the year."

questions.title: Pearce believes that his building would be improved by

options.text: becoming more of a habitat for wildlife.
even closer links with the history of Zimbabwe.
giving people more space to interact with nature.

questions.analysis:
题干要求我们选择一个选项，以改进Pearce的建筑。为了快速找到答案，我们可以按照以下步骤进行定位：

1. 首先，我们看到题干中提到了Pearce，我们可以在原文中找到与之相关的信息。原文中提到了"he"，这指的就是Pearce。

2. 其次，题干中提到了"building"，我们可以在原文中找到与之相关的信息。原文中提到了"it"，这指的就是Pearce的建筑。

3. 最后，题干中提到了"improved"，我们可以在原文中找到与之相关的信息。原文中提到了"Pearce said he hoped plants would grow wild in the atrium and pigeons and bats would move into it"，这表明Pearce希望在建筑中有野生植物生长，并且希望鸽子和蝙蝠能够进入其中。

综上所述，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出答案为"A. becoming more of a habitat for wildlife."，即建筑变得更适合野生动物栖息。

collect_option.title: Which three parts of the Eastgate Building reflect important features of Zimbabwe's history and culture?

questions.analysis:
题目要求我们选择三个反映津巴布韦历史和文化重要特征的东方大厦的部分。我们可以通过以下步骤快速找到答案：

1. 首先，我们需要找到关于东方大厦的信息。根据题干中的定位词"Eastgate Building"，我们可以在原文中找到对应的信息："Pearce, disdaining smooth glass skins as ‘igloos in the Sahara’, calls his building, with its exposed girders and pipes, ‘spiky’." 这句话提到了"his building"，即东方大厦。

2. 接下来，我们需要找到关于东方大厦的不同部分的描述。根据题干中的定位词"parts"，我们可以在原文中找到对应的信息。原文中提到了三个部分：
 - "The design of the entrances is based on the porcupine-quill headdresses of the local Shona tribe." 这句话描述了入口的设计，它基于当地Shona部落的豪猪刺头饰。
 - "Elevators are designed to look like the mineshaft cages used in Zimbabwe's diamond mines." 这句话描述了电梯的设计，它们看起来像是津巴布韦钻石矿中使用的井笼。
 - "The shape of the fan covers, and the stone used in their construction, are echoes of Great Zimbabwe, the ruins that give the country its name." 这句话描述了风扇罩的形状和构造中使用的石头，它们是津巴布韦的名字来源——大津巴布韦遗址的回响。

3. 最后，我们需要确定哪些部分反映了津巴布韦的历史和文化重要特征。根据原文中的描述，我们可以得出以下结论：
 - 入口的设计基于当地Shona部落的豪猪刺头饰，这反映了津巴布韦的文化特征。
 - 电梯的设计模仿了津巴布韦钻石矿中使用的井笼，这反映了津巴布韦的历史和经济特征。
 - 风扇罩的形状和构造中使用的石头是对大津巴布韦遗址的回响，这也反映了津巴布韦的历史和文化特征。

因此，答案是"entrances, elevators, fan covers"，这三个部分反映了津巴布韦的历史和文化重要特征。

content.title: Why are so few tigers man-eaters?
content.summary: {'A': '尽管印度的老虎每天都能看到人类，但对人类的攻击并不频繁，这一点有些奇怪。', 'B': '这段话主要讲述了一些人可能会争辩说过去这些袭击实际上很常见，但是他们可能夸大了老虎所带来的恐怖，因为过去老虎数量更多，所以相对来说袭击事件在过去和现在一样罕见。', 'C': '这段话主要讲述了关于老虎害怕的原因的讨论，作者认为解释可能更简单且更有趣。', 'D': '自从20世纪50年代以来，我们一直试图从动物的角度来理解动物行为。然而，在该领域的先驱者如康拉德·洛伦兹等人进行的第一批优雅实验之前，博物学家们将动物描述得好像它们是稍微不那么聪明的人类。相比之下，行为学的原则要求我们尝试以我们正在研究的动物的思维方式来思考，并且观察其行为的每一个细节，而不将我们自己的人类意义强加于其行动之上。', 'E': '这段话主要讲述了老虎对人类的恐惧不是基于祖先的逻辑，而是基于老虎对人类的视觉感知，从而解释了为什么老虎对人类的反应会有所不同。', 'F': '蹲着的人更容易成为老虎的攻击目标，与站着的人相比，蹲着的人更像中等大小的鹿，因此更吸引老虎的注意。', 'G': '人类直立行走不仅是与其他物种的区别，还是帮助他们在危险和不可预测的环境中生存的因素。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: As you leave the Bandhavgarh National Park in central India, there is a notice which shows a huge, placid tiger. The notice says, 'You may not have seen me, but I have seen you.' There are more than a billion people In India and Indian tigers probably see humans every single day of their lives. Tigers can and do kill almost everything they meet in the jungle, they will kill even attack elephants and rhino. Surely, then, it is a little strange that attacks on humans are not more frequent.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: Some people might argue that these attacks were in fact common in the past. British writers of adventure stories, such as Jim Corbett, gave the impression that village life in India in the early years of the twentieth century involved a stage of constant siege by man-eating tigers. But they may have overstated the terror spread by tigers. There were also far more tigers around in those days (probably 60.000 in the subcontinent compared to just 3000 today). So in proportion, attacks appear to have been as rare then as they are today.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: It is widely assumed that the constraint is fear; but what exactly are tigers afraid of? Can they really know that we may be even better armed than they are? Surely not. Has the species programmed the experiences of all tigers with humans its genes to be inherited as instinct? Perhaps. But I think the explanation may be more simple and, in a way, more intriguing.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Since the growth of ethology in the 1950s. we have tried to understand animal behaviour from the animal's point of view. Until the first elegant experiments by pioneers in the field such as Konrad Lorenz, naturalists wrote about animals as if they were slightly less intelligent humans. Jim Corbett's breathless accounts of his duels with a an-eaters in truth tell us more about Jim Corbett than they do about the animals. The principle of ethology, on the other hand, requires us to attempt to think in the same way as the animal we are studying thinks, and to observe every tiny detail of its behaviour without imposing our own human significances on its actions.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: I suspect that a tiger's afraid of humans lies not in some preprogramed ancestral logic but in the way he actually perceives us visually. If you think like a tiger, a human in a car might appear just to be a part of the car, and because tigers don't eat cars the human is safe-unless the car is menacing the tiger or its cubs, in which case a brave or enraged tiger may charge. A human on foot is a different sort of puzzle. Imagine a tiger sees a man who is 1.8m tall. A tiger is less than 1m tall but they may be up to 3m long from head to tail. So when a tiger sees the man face on, it might not be unreasonable for him to assume that the man is 6m long. If he meet a deer of this size, he might attack the animal by leaping on its back, but when he looks behind the mind he can't see a back. From the front the man is huge, but looked at from the side he all but disappears. This must be very disconcerting. A hunter has to be confident that it can tackle its prey, and no one is confident when they are disconcerted. This is especially true of a solitary hunter such as the tiger and may explain why lions-particularly young lionesses who tend to encourage one another to take risks are more dangerous than tigers.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: If the theory that a tiger is disconcerted to find that a standing human is both very big and yet somehow invisible is correct, the opposite should be true of a squatting human. A squatting human is half he size and presents twice the spread of back, and more closely resembles a medium-sized deer. If tigers were simply frightened of all humans, then a squatting person would be no more attractive as a target than a standing one. This, however appears not to be the case. Many incidents of attacks on people involving villagers squatting or bending over to cut grass for fodder or building material.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text:
The fact that humans stand upright may therefore not just be something that distinguishes them from nearly all other species, but also a factor that helped them to survive in a dangerous and unpredictable environment.
Note:
Ethology = the branch of zoology that studies the behaviour of animals in their natural habitats

questions.analysis:
题干中提到"Some writers of fiction have exaggerated the danger of tigers to man."，我们需要在文章中找到相关信息来判断这个说法是否正确。

在原文中，有一句话提到了British writers of adventure stories，他们给人们留下了一个印象，即在20世纪初的印度，村庄生活似乎一直处于被食人虎围困的状态。但是，作者也提到这些作家可能夸大了虎对人类的恐怖传播。

所以，根据原文中的信息，我们可以得出结论，一些小说作家夸大了虎对人类的危险。因此，答案是"TRUE"。

questions.title: Why do tigers rarely attack people in cars?

options.text: They have learned that cars are not dangerous.
They realise that people in cars cannot be harmed.
They do not think people in cars are living creatures.
They do not want to put their cubs at risk

questions.analysis:
根据题干"Why do tigers rarely attack people in cars?"，我们可以通过以下步骤快速找到答案：

1. 首先，我们需要找到与题干中的关键词相关的原文信息。题干中的关键词是"tigers"和"in cars"。

2. 在原文中，我们可以找到与"tigers"相关的同义表达"tiger's"，出现在句子"I suspect that a tiger’s afraid of humans lies not in some preprogramed ancestral logic but in the way he actually perceives us visually."。

3. 在同一句子中，我们可以找到与"in cars"相关的同义表达"in a car"，出现在句子"If you think like a tiger, a human in a car might appear just to be a part of the car, and because tigers don’t eat cars the human is safe-unless the car is menacing the tiger or its cubs, in which case a brave or enraged tiger may charge."。

4. 根据原文信息，我们可以得出结论：老虎很少攻击坐在车里的人，因为老虎认为人类在车里只是车的一部分，而老虎不吃车，所以人类是安全的。

5. 根据以上分析，我们可以选择答案C："They do not think people in cars are living creatures."，这个选项与我们的结论一致。

因此，答案为C，老虎很少攻击坐在车里的人，是因为他们不认为坐在车里的人是活物。

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: When Peter Osbeck. a Swedish priest, stopped off at the mid-Atlantic island of Ascension in 1752 on his way home from China, he wrote of 'a heap of ruinous rocks' with a bare, white mountain in the middle. All it boasted was a couple of dozen species of plant, most of them ferns and some of them unique to the island.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: And so it might have remained. But in 1843 British plant collector Joseph Hooker made a brief call on his return from Antarctica. Surveying the bare earth, he concluded that the island had suffered some natural calamity that had denuded it of vegetation and triggered a decline in rainfall that was turning the place into a desert. The British Navy, which by then maintained a garrison on the island, was keen to improve the place and asked Hooker's advice. He suggested an ambitious scheme for planting trees and shrubs that would revive rainfall and stimulate a wider ecological recovery. And, perhaps lacking anything else to do, the sailors set to with a will.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: In 1845, a naval transport ship from Argentina delivered a batch of seedlings. In the following years, more than 200 species of plant arrived from South Africa, from England came 700 packets of seeds, including those of two species that especially liked the place: bamboo and prickly pear. With sailors planting several thousand trees a year, the bare white mountain was soon cloaked in green and renamed Green Mountain, and by the early twentieth century the mountain's slopes were covered with a variety of trees and shrubs from all over the world.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Modern ecologists throw up their hands in horror at what they see as Hookers environmental anarchy. The exotic species wrecked the indigenous ecosystem, squeezing out the islands endemic plants. In fact. Hooker knew well enough what might happen. However, he saw greater benefit in improving rainfall and encouraging more prolific vegetation on the island.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: But there is a much deeper issue here than the relative benefits of sparse endemic species versus luxuriant imported ones. And as botanist David Wilkinson of Liverpool John Moores University in the UK pointed out after a recent visit to the island, it goes to the heart of some of the most dearly held tenets of ecology. Conservationists' understandable concern for the fate of Ascension's handful of unique species has, he says, blinded them to something quite astonishing the fact that the introduced species have been a roaring success.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Today's Green Mountain, says Wilkinson, is 'a fully functioning man-made tropical cloud forest'that has grown from scratch from a ragbag of species collected more or less at random from all over the planet. But how could it have happened? Conventional ecological theory says that complex ecosystems such as cloud forests can emerge only through evolutionary processes in which each organism develops in concert with others to fill particular niches. Plants eo-evolve with their pollinators and seed dispersers, while microbes in the soil evolve to deal with the leaf litter.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: But that's not what happened on Green Mountain. And the experience suggests that perhaps natural rainforests are constructed far more by chance than by evolution. Species, say some ecologists, don't so much evolve to create ecosystems as make the best of what they have. 'The Green Mountain system is a man-made system that has produced a tropical rainforest without any co-evolution between its constituent species,' says Wilkinson.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Not everyone agrees. Alan Gray, an ecologist at the University of Edinburgh in the UK. argues that the surviving endemic species on Green Mountain, though small in number, may still form the framework of the new' ecosystem. The new arrivals may just be an adornment, with little structural importance for the ecosystem.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: But to Wilkinson this sounds like clutching at straws. And the idea of the instant formation of rainforests sounds increasingly plausible as research reveals that supposedly pristine tropical rainforests from the Amazon to south-east Asia may in places be little more titan the overgrown gardens of past rainforest civilisations.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: The most surprising thing of all is that no ecologists have thought to conduct proper research into this human-made rainforest ecosystem. A survey of the island's flora conducted six years ago by the University of Edinburgh was concerned only with endemic species. They characterised everything else as a threat. And the Ascension authorities are currently turning Green Mountain into a national park where introduced species, at least the invasive ones, are earmarked for culling rather than conservation.

paragraphs.label: K
paragraphs.text: Conservationists have understandable concerns, Wilkinson says. At least four endemic species have gone extinct on Ascension since the exotics started arriving. But in their urgency to protect endemics, ecologists are missing out on the study of a great enigma.

paragraphs.label: L
paragraphs.text: 'As you walk through the forest, you see lots of leaves that have had chunks taken out of them by various insects. There are caterpillars and beetles around.' says Wilkinson. 'But where did they come from? Are they endemic or alien? If alien, did they come with the plant on which they feed or discover it on arrival?' Such questions go to the heart of how- rainforests happen.

paragraphs.label: M
paragraphs.text: The Green Mountain forest holds many secrets. And the irony is that the most artificial rainforest in the world could tell us more about rainforest ecology than any number of natural forests.
